FULL CONTENT:
# [Tennessee Council for Professional Archaeology](https://tennesseearchaeologycouncil.wordpress.com/)

# [Like the TCPA on Facebook!](https://www.facebook.com/Tennessee.Archaeology)

![](https://tennesseearchaeologycouncil.wordpress.com/wp-content/uploads/2015/11/cropped-2015_logo.jpg)

# [Tennessee Council for Professional Archaeology](https://tennesseearchaeologycouncil.wordpress.com/)

## Promoting professionalism in the field of archaeology, archaeological awareness, and stewardship of our past

# [Like the TCPA on Facebook!](https://www.facebook.com/Tennessee.Archaeology)

# Creating a Sustainable Public Archaeology Program at the Lone Rock Stockade through Community Partnerships

Camille Westmont

The University of the South

Public archaeology has a major problem: sustainability. All too often, archaeologists come into a community, set up a public or community archaeology project, things go well for a few years, and then the archaeologist moves on to another project, taking their field-specific knowledge with them and leaving the community oftentimes unable to continue the project themselves. This can not only be frustrating for the communities left behind—it can also cause real harm (McAnany and Parks 2012; Pyburn 2014).

When I began my archival and archaeological investigations into the Lone Rock Stockade in late 2019, I knew that I wanted community and public archaeology to be a prominent component of the project. However, as a temporary faculty member, I wasn’t sure how to address this issue of sustainability. I received a grant from the Tennessee Civil War National Heritage Area to support public outreach and I began looking forward to summer 2020 when I would be able to invite people out to the site to learn, excavate, and interpret the history of convict leasing in southern Middle Tennessee. As the COVID-19 pandemic began to shutter plans, however, those dreams changed.

With the 2020 University of the South Field School in Archaeology cancelled, it looked doubtful that I would be able to carry out excavations at the site. Suddenly, only a few weeks before the field season had been supposed to start, I received an email from Sewanee’s AmeriCorps VISTA program. Their summer plans, too, had been impacted by the COVID-19 pandemic, and they were looking for outdoor activities for the VISTA volunteers to participate in. While not originally in the plan, the AmeriCorps VISTA program proved to be a perfect partner for the project. The archaeological project provided VISTA volunteers with a full-time work opportunity located outdoors; many tasks (such as screening) could be completed while socially distanced; the work could be paused to accommodate VISTA volunteers’ other obligations to the AmeriCorps VISTA program; and the archaeology project could easily accommodate a variable work crew. In return, the AmeriCorps VISTA program provided the archaeological project with a reliable field crew for the summer, and took over all of the logistics for paying, housing, and hiring the crew (Figures 1 and 2).

![](https://tennesseearchaeologycouncil.wordpress.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/09/thumbnail_picture13.png?w=731)
![](https://tennesseearchaeologycouncil.wordpress.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/09/thumbnail_picture15.png?w=733)

The first year of the partnership, summer 2020, saw only a handful of VISTA volunteers at the site. Limitations on crew size were required to help reduce the risk of COVID and ensure volunteers did not need to share equipment. These initial VISTA volunteers received hands-on training in archaeological excavation methods and learned more about the region’s history of convict leasing. Gradually, the VISTA volunteers began playing a more active role in the public outreach at the site, including assisting on tours and on public excavation days.

This limited interaction in 2020 has steadily grown over the last year. In February 2021, a group of approximately 20 AmeriCorps VISTA-affiliated volunteers participated in a virtual Transcribe-a-Thon to help transcribe the names of the individuals imprisoned at the Lone Rock Stockade in honor of Martin Luther King Jr. Day. Through their efforts, we were able to transcribe nearly 1,000 names in a single afternoon. In summer 2021, the VISTA volunteers once again assisted with the excavations at the site—this time alongside students participating in the

University of the South’s 2021 Field School in Archaeology. One VISTA volunteer who participated in excavations in 2020 returned to volunteer at the site again in 2021. Additionally, one VISTA volunteer assisted the effort remotely by helping to standardize and correct the crowd-sourced transcribed data, making it more searchable for individuals looking for their ancestors.

The unanticipated partnership between the Lone Rock Stockade Archaeological Project and Sewanee’s AmeriCorps VISTA program is training local youth leaders in the archaeological and archival skills necessary to carry on the project after I leave. What began as a convenient arrangement for both now has the potential to turn into a truly sustainable program adopted and maintained by local community members. While we still have a long way to go to ensure the project can continue its work, this type of partnership has already made major inroads in compensating community members while they learn archaeological techniques, thereby removing one barrier to participation; engaging with local youth; and providing in-depth training on a largely hidden form of local history. Moving forward, I hope to continue developing archaeological capacities locally to ensure the continued longevity of this project.

**References:**

McAnany, P. A. and Parks, S. (2012). Casualties of heritage distancing: children, Ch’orti’ Indigeneity, and the Copán archaeoscape. Current Anthropology 53(1): 80-107.

Pyburn, K. A. (2014). Preservation as “disaster capitalism”: the downside of site rescue and the complexity of community engagement. Public Archaeology 13(1-3): 226-239

The Laboratory's approach to community engagement is multilayered and includes shared stewardship, multi-vocal approaches, two-way communication, trust,

FULL CONTENT:
![Home](/sites/default/files/screen-as-anthro-lbarch-h-fc.png)

# Descendant Community Engagement

The Laboratory’s approach to community engagement is multilayered and includes shared stewardship, multi-vocal approaches, two-way communication, trust, capacity building, equitable partnerships, knowledge sharing, transparency, and joint decision-making. We refer to this as descendant community-informed institutional integrity (DCIII).

By recognizing that the Lab houses collections that are the intellectual and cultural property of many different descendant communities, we have shifted to practices that include the active exchange of ideas and the development of mutual respect the centers on correcting past mistakes and views within archaeology.

![MCN-box](/sites/default/files/styles/landscape/public/images/MCN_Box.png?h=1fad84bd&itok=BdIBWq1x)

### 

### **What Is a Descendant Community?**

A "descendant community" within the Laboratory can mean many things, including, but not limited to, a tribal nation or an organization or family representing a diasporic or other community of origin. A descendant community retains ancestral and cultural ties to collections stewarded by the Laboratory.

### 

### **Deep Collaboration and Research Integrity**

The Lab’s implementation of our mission, in practice, now centers on high integrity through deep collaboration where our descendant community partners and archaeologists are equal decision-makers. 

One way we have incorporated this is through the Lab's new policies and procedures that were revised with descendant community input, including **[NAGPRA](//archaeology.uga.edu/nagpra)** policies and a revised **[collections management policy](//efaidnbmnnnibpcajpcglclefindmkaj/https://archaeology.uga.edu/sites/default/files/documents/2022_uga_collectionsmanagement_policy.pdf)**. We have also established advisory boards comprised of descendent community representatives, Laboratory directors, and university faculty.

![EBCI-box](/sites/default/files/styles/landscape/public/images/EasternBand_Box.png?h=206f4af6&itok=guTn2Isv)

### **“Open-Door” Philosophy**

Through ongoing efforts in building relationships and trust, the Lab has established an “open-door” philosophy with descendent communities concerning projects and collections. Regular communication and transparency concerning our work at the Lab is essential to ensure descendent community representation in decision-making and cooperative planning.

### **Institutional Change**

These partnerships have directly allowed for the integration of descendant community perspectives in all aspects of lab management and policies, including research access, education and public engagement, and curation and collections management.

Through collaboration, we can foster a community of university-wide institutional integrity with respect for descendent communities and their histories.

![BrownTransfer-box](/sites/default/files/styles/landscape/public/images/JBrownTransfer_Caption.png?h=75b873d8&itok=4qusKUjd)

### **Centering on People and Histories, Not Objects**

Lived people of the past should not be viewed as different from or in separation of the descendants living today. Descendant community engagement has enhanced our ability to be a more conscientious organization that centers on people and their histories, not merely the objects of scientific study.

Our journey is ongoing, and we hope that the Lab will always retain this evolving and multivocal flow regarding the input of Indigenous and other descendant community perspectives and knowledge.

## [Descendant Community Engagement](/descendant-community-engagement)

## SUPPORT ARCHAEOLOGY

Help us to continue to make advances in research, stewardship, and bringing the past to life. ![](https://press.ierek.com/public/journals/2/pageHeaderLogoImage_en_US.png)

# The Key Role of Community Archaeology in Enhancing Local Identity and Territorial Bonds in Turkey and Jordan

![](https://crossmark-cdn.crossref.org/widget/v2.0/logos/CROSSMARK_Color_horizontal.svg)

## Article Sidebar

## Abstract

The present research study, part of an ongoing doctoral project, is an interpretative-comparative analysis conducted considering two geographically distinct contexts: the archaeological site of Arslantepe with its village of Orduzu (Malatya) in Turkey, and the archaeological site of Tell Hisban with its village of Hisban (Amman), in Jordan. In both cases, the site is situated almost at the centre of the anthropic and natural landscape, forming a historical and natural monument. A qualitative research survey was carried out through interviews with semi-structured questions directed towards the resident population, to understand: the vision of the site, the level of involvement, the impact on society, and the relationship with the archaeological team. The research aims to understand whether community archaeology applications can contribute to increasing the recognition of the archaeological site as a fundamental part of their territory. The result is that such practices can foster a stronger connection with the past and the contemporary landscape, contributing to the transformation of the area by enhancing local well-being and benefits in sustainable development. This study is entered into the field of Community archaeology that, by establishing continuous communication between archaeologists, the local community, and their cultural heritage, and incorporating the local perspective into the interpretation of the past, aims to contribute to increasing awareness of the cultural heritage and its incorporation within the local identity.

## Full text article

## Article Details

![Crossref](https://press.ierek.com/plugins/generic/citations/images/crossref.png)
![Scopus](https://press.ierek.com/plugins/generic/citations/images/scopus.png)
![Google Scholar](https://press.ierek.com/plugins/generic/citations/images/scholar.png)
![Europe PMC](https://press.ierek.com/plugins/generic/citations/images/pmc.png)

## 1. Introduction

Archaeological sites and artifacts are often deeply intertwined with the landscapes where people live today. This relationship between heritage and place is crucial, as it connects the past with contemporary life through the people who have inhabited the territory over time. Furthermore, the definition of heritage and landscapes is not an objective practice. They are shaped by the perspectives and values of those involved. Community archaeology, by including local communities in these processes, aims to transform them no longer into mere passive observers, but into active participants in the documentation, interpretation, and management of their cultural heritage. This approach enriches archaeological studies by integrating local knowledge and contemporary perspectives, deepening the understanding of past landscapes, and ensuring a more inclusive and relevant interpretation of history[(Álvarez Larrain & McCall, 2019)](#BIBR-1). According to this perspective, cultural heritage, including archaeological sites, gains significance and a future only if its social value is maintained. Preservation involves more than protecting physical remains; it requires awareness by the community of the cultural significance, which must actively participate in its preservation and promotion[(Manacorda, 2007)](#BIBR-15). This study, through the analysis of qualitative survey data, demonstrates how community archaeology and the active involvement of residents can increase the recognition of the archaeological site as a fundamental component of their local landscape.

## 2. Literature review

The present study falls within the field of community archaeology, a multifaceted discipline encompassing various aspects of archaeological theory and practice. Its primary goal is to establish an active relationship with contemporary society, which goes beyond mere communication and the transmission of knowledge by specialists. Rather, it is based on constant interaction, active involvement, and the participation of local communities in the definition and interpretation of their own past[(Corbishley, 2011)](#BIBR-5)[(Moser et al., 2002)](#BIBR-23). Community archaeology is thus central to the critical evaluation of archaeology in the modern world, particularly in how archaeologists engage with the public, encompassing themes of economic, social, political, religious, and environmental relevance[(Moshenska, 2017)](#BIBR-21). The community has both the duty and the right to express curiosity, passion, and pride toward its cultural heritage, which is a fundamental part of its territory[(Roberts, 2017)](#BIBR-24). This identification and strengthening of the connection with heritage is only achievable through multi-layered communication between archaeologists and the local community, and the creation of a direct, bidirectional dialogue. This dialogue must be based on the sharing of knowledge and the integration of local wisdom with archaeological expertise, achieved through inclusive and collaborative practices. The initial phase of formalized community projects dates to the 1980s, emerging from the idea of amateur involvement in archaeological research in the United Kingdom. As it developed further, this original concept of "community-led" archaeology sparked intense debate regarding its definition[(Thomas, 2017)](#BIBR-27). Over time, archaeologists around the world have engaged with many different communities and sectors of society, following a wide variety of theoretical models and/or applied methods, depending on local traditions, economic, political, and social realities, as well as how archaeology is perceived in each nation. In some cases, it refers to a collaborative research method between communities and professionals, while in others, it describes a practice facilitated and guided by professionals, where the public is not necessarily the creator of the information provided. In other cases, the concept of community archaeology is framed as "archaeology by the people for the people"[(Community archaeology: Themes, 2012)](#BIBR-22)or "archaeology from below"[(Faulkner, 2000)](#BIBR-6). These expressions have been misappropriated over time, misrepresenting the scholarly understanding behind them. In fact, the interpretation of community archaeology is the result of continuous and distinct development in the various countries where it is applied[(Thomas, 2017)](#BIBR-27). It responds to specific needs and motivations within each individual context, which frequently lead to different nuances in its theoretical and methodological definition (indigenous archaeology, community-based participatory research, collaborative archeology, co-creation archaeology, civic engagement). As a consequence, some researchers have stressed the necessity of developing a global perspective to understand how each country has incorporated and implemented community archeology in its projects. Okamura and Mastuda[(Matsuda & Okamura, 2011)](#BIBR-17)proposed an implementation of the Merriman's and Holtorf's earlier theories on public archaeology (2004; 2007), termed the Multiple Perspective Model with a Multivocal Approach. This more theory-oriented approach considers the public as a subject, possessing its own agency that interacts with the past according to its beliefs, interests, and needs. The added Multivocal Approach aims to identify and recognize the various interpretations of archaeological materials made by different social groups or individuals across various contemporary contexts[(Matsuda, 2016)](#BIBR-18). It is essential to assess the extent to which community archeology has been adopted and applied in different countries and how it has become integrated into archaeological practice in each context. This model with its perspective serves as the theoretical foundation of the present research on community archaeology, acknowledging the diversity of interests, attitudes, demands, and needs expressed by various individuals and accepting multiple forms of engagement with the past.

It is important to highlight how, in many archaeological excavations, although not always in a formalized manner, contact with the community has played a crucial role in archaeological activities since the beginning of the excavation experience. Conversely, the recent need to formalize community engagement in archaeology projects pg. 2 has, in some cases, led to a shift in priorities that potentially diminishes the significance of this interaction. In some contexts

enriched_points.expanded_content: Building lasting relationships with communities through ongoing projects, like the Lone Rock Stockade initiative, ensures knowledge transfer and empowers locals to maintain archaeological efforts.

Utilizing technology and creative outreach, such as virtual events and participatory mapping, enhances public involvement and promotes collaborative interpretations of heritage, as seen in community archaeology projects.

Emphasizing diverse voices and perspectives in archaeological narratives fosters a sense of belonging, as demonstrated by descendant community engagement practices that prioritize mutual respect and shared decision-making.
